description: This is the second of two capstone courses that together will provide a field-based action learning opportunity to analyze problems of practice using multiple frames to develop meaningful solutions.  Similar to the dissertation, the Capstone Project should be a demonstration of the student's ability to perform disciplined inquiry and in accordance with traditional standards for doctoral performance.

title: RESEARCH TEACHING&LEARNING
description: This course examines policy and research in teaching and learning, including relationships among research and educational policy for teaching and teacher education. It is intensive study in an area of curriculum, teaching, and learning; educational policy and social analysis; and teacher education and teacher learning.

title: DISSERTATION SEMINAR
description: Assesses researchable questions in leadership and administration in a student's area of specialization resulting in a dissertation proposal.

title: RHETORICAL ANALYSIS
description: In-depth analysis of advertisements, speeches, film, and  literature from a rhetorical perspective.

title: LITERATURE AND FILM
description: Examination of film history and film forms as part of a larger cultural history. Clarification and differentiation of the connections between film and literature. Exploration of the ways literary concepts are interpreted through film.

title: PRACTI INQ:IDENT,FRAM, INV PROB PRACTIC
description: Practitioner Inquiry I is the initial course of five courses in the inquiry sequence that introduces students to Problems of Practice through a variety of educational research and evaluation perspectives, approaches, and methods. This course provides a foundation for further participatory action research (PAR) and return on investment evaluation.

title: ENGLISH STUDIES:
description: Selected literary topics, figures and genres.

title: 20TH CENT LIT:1900-1945
description: Selected works of the twentieth century.

title: AMERICAN LIT:1800-1865
description: Selected readings from major writers of the period.

title: CAPSTONE PROJ: DISSERTATION PRACTICE I
description: This is the first of two capstone courses that together will provide a field-based action learning opportunity to analyze problems of practice using multiple frames to develop meaningful solutions.  Similar to the dissertation, the Capstone Project should be a demonstration of the student's ability to perform disciplined inquiry in accordance with traditional standards for doctoral performance.

title: TCH ENG MID&SEC SCH 6-12
description: Problems confronting teachers of English in the secondary school, current research, organization of courses, sources of materials and textbooks, and methods of teaching.  Prerequisite:   Candidacy in the School of Education.

title: POETRY
description: Studies in poetry through various works, authors, and periods.

title: PRACTICUM
description: Creation of a portfolio or major document demonstrating grasp of the writing process, from theory through research, to  writing and extracompostional enhancement.

title: SHAKESPEARE
description: Selected Shakespearean plays, studies in relation to Shakespeare's art.

title: COMPARATIVE LITERATURE
description: Introduction to the comparative study of literary forms and forms in the other arts.

title: CONTINUOUS MATRICULATION
description: Enrollment is required after the completion of ADL 798 and  799 each semester until the dissertation is successfully  defended.

title: TECH TEACHING COMP
description: Writing as a mode of learning; integrating instruction in reading and writing; creating and implementing a course.

title: INTERNSHIP
description: Work in a writing capacity for a private or public company.

title: MASTER'S THESIS
description: Research investigation or analytical study of a work of literature.

title: STUDY DESIGNS IN EPIDEMIOLOGY
description: Epidemiology is the identification of causes of disease that can guide the development of rational public health policies. This course examines the methods used in epidemiologic research, including the design, collection, and analysis of accurate data. The emphasis of the course is on knowledge and skills required to develop and conduct studies up to the point of conducting primary data analyses.

title: 20TH CENT LIT:1945-PRESENT
description: Selected works of the period.

title: DIRECTED RESEARCH
description: Opportunity to pursue independent research under the guidance of an advisor from the Department.

title: LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT AND COMMUNICATION SKILLS DEFICITS IN EXCEPTIONAL CHILDREN
description: Identifies language development and communication  deficits and selects appropriate instruction to  meet individual student needs across educational  settings.

title: THEO/RES IN LEARNING DIS
description: Examines theories in reading and related learning disabilities through physiological, psychological and sensory correlates. Includes an investigation of recent research conducted in  relation to success and failure in teaching the child with  reading and related learning disabilities.

title: TRANSITION:INTERPERSONAL AND POST- SECONDARY LIKE SKILLS TO STUDENTS WITH EXCEPTIONALITIES
description: Evaluates special needs of Exceptional Students in the areas of employability skills, career awareness and transition planning for adult living.

title: CURR/INSTRUCT'L MATERIAL
description: Analyzes and applies specific teaching methods and curriculum materials for individualizing remedial instruction for pupils with reading and related learning disabilities.  Includes  classroom management skills.

title: EDUC SPEC GIFTED POPULAT
description: Examines the education of special gifted populations including, but not limited to minorities, underachievers, disabled/ handicapped, economically disadvantaged and highly gifted to include student characteristics and programmatic adaptations.

title: PRINCIPLES EPIDEMIOLOGY PUBLIC HEALTH
description: In this introductory course students will apply basic concepts of epidemiology to multiple domains of public health. Students learn how to use Epidemiology to better understand and promote health at a population level.

title: PHYSIO HEAR&SPEECH MECHANISMS
description: Examines the physics of sound, anatomy of the speech/hearing mechanisms. Provides an overview of audiometric testing, assistive listening devices, and classroom acoustics. Eight hours of field experience required.

title: INSTR STRAT MNTL RETARDATION
description: Examines and evaluates instructional strategies for the educable, trainable and profoundly mentally handicapped based on the  development, implementation and evaluation of individualized education plans.

title: EPIDEMIOLOGY METHODS II
description: This course develops epidemiologic concepts introduced in EPID Epidemiologic Methods I. Students expand research skills by exploring models, causality, bias, study design, interaction, effect, modification, and mediation while analyzing public health data sets. The course covers concepts, methods, and application of key modeling approaches to evaluation of multivariate data from epidemiologic studies.

title: MGMT OF STU W/EXCEP
description: Analyzes the various forms of classroom organization, behavior  management and communication skills appropriate for managing the  spectrum of exceptional students.

title: EPIDEMIOLOGY METHODS I
description: This course introduces students to intermediate analytical techniques for understanding and using epidemiological research. Students learn principles and methods of data analysis that are applied in population health management. Students develop skills in the translation of research such as summary findings, data displays, use of classical statistics approaches, and impact of ethical issues.

title: SIGN LANGUAGE I
description: Explores the historical development of manual communication, studies the relationship between manually coded English and American Sign Language, deaf culture, and the implementation of signs in a variety of settings. Eight hours of field experience; minimum of 8 hours language lab required.

title: INSTRUCTIONAL STRATEGIES FOR STUDETNS WITH LEARNING DISIBILITIES
description: Surveys instructional techniques for teaching children with learning disabilities, including specialized approaches in basic skills and adaptation of curriculum materials. Supervised field experience required.

title: GUI/COU OF GIFTED STU
description: Focuses on the guidance and counseling of exceptional children, including special personality needs and strategies for  invention.

title: THEO/RES OF STU EMOTION HND
description: Examines the nature and needs of the emotionally handicapped to include etiology, prevention and intervention, as well as use of community resources.

title: EDU/HEARING IMPAIRED
description: Introduces the student to an overview of different language development techniques for the hearing impaired child beginning with normal language development.  It also covers the social and psychological elements influencing the hearing impaired child.

title: YOUNG HANDICAP CHILD II
description: Focuses on the cognitive and social development of the young  handicapped child.  Information on language disorders, visual and hearing impairments will be studied.

title: EDU CHILD W/VISUAL DIS
description: Examines the population defined as blind or partially sighted. It is directed at those aspects of behavior that are affected  by loss of vision and appropriate delivery systems of  educational or rehabilitation services.

title: TYPICAL AND ATYPICAL EARLY DEVELOPMENT
description: Focuses on the development of young children with disabilities (ages 0-5). Children's normal development, genetic, and environmental factors that contribute to various disabilities, and the impact of disabilities on development and learning will be studied. Includes medical information regarding the etiologies of various disabilities.

title: NATURE/NEEDS OF GIFTED
description: Introduces the student to  the characteristics and special needs of the gifted child.  The unique nature and nurture situations in the development of the gifted child are discussed.

title: THEORY/DEV CREATIVITY
description: Examines theory and development of creativity to include elements of creativity such as fluency, flexibility, originality and  elaboration.

title: BEH MGT/POS BEH SUP CHD W/AUT SPEC DIS
description: This course compares various behavioral management techniques appropriate for children with autism and children with emotional handicaps. A minimum of eight (8) hours of field experience required.

title: INSTRUC STRAT/EMOTION/BEH DISOR
description: Surveys instructional strategies for teaching students who are identified as emotionally handicapped, including instructional techniques, motivational strategies, development, implementation and evaluation of individualized education plans as well as data base management.

title: CLASS MGT-EMOTION HAND
description: Compares various classroom management techniques and specific situations within that classroom involving the management of emotionally handicapped children.

title: ED PROC/CURR GIFTED
description: Analyzes the procedures, curriculum and specific teaching models for gifted children.  An emphasis on individual and group needs through curriculum and materials will be introduced.

title: FOUND MNTL HANDICAP
description: Examines the biological, psychological and social foundations of mental deficiency, with emphasis on the need for educating the  community to greater understanding and increased provision for  mentally deficient children and adults.

title: EDU PROG/EMOTIONALLY HAN
description: Appraises the curriculum and program for the emotionally handicapped child. Programs for the multi-handicapped child with dominant emotional problems will be evaluated.  The  individual program as it affects the group curriculum will be analyzed.

title: ADVANCED LEARNING STRATEGIES FOR STUDENTS WITH EXCEPTIONALITIES
description: Focuses on research-based principles, instructional procedures, and organizational techniques that enables students with learning disabilities to learn, to solve problems, and to complete tasks independently.

title: TEACH INTERN: ESE
description: Provides the graduate intern Exceptional Student Education teacher with school-site teaching experience under the supervision of a certified teacher and University personnel. Prerequisites: 3.00 GPA; must be eligible for K-12 Exceptional Student Education Certification.

title: EDU ASSESS OF STU W/EXCEPT
description: Proposes educational assessment techniques and procedures to  provide an objective data base for individualizing instruction for exceptional children.

title: ASSESSMENT AND INTERVENTION IN PREKINDERGARTEN SPECIAL EDITION
description: Selects assessment instruments useful in the evaluation of infants and young children. Formal  and informal evaluation techniques are  emphasized, as well as the use of assessment to inform program planning.  The need to develop multidisciplinary teams for

title: EDU MGMT OF STU W/MENTAL DISB
description: Surveys the education, management and curriculum of mentally handicapped (educable, trainable and profoundly handicapped) with emphasis on individual as well as classroom management techniques.

title: EVAL CHLDRN W/LRN DISAB
description: Evaluates the instructional diagnosis of the learning disabled child with formal and informal diagnostic procedures used to  determine causes and nature of learning disabilities. Specific recommendations for individual education programs to ameliorate learning problems in home, school and community will be  discussed.

title: COLLABORATIVE PRACTICES IN INCLUSIVE CURRICULUM DESIGN
description: Concentrates on the development of the most appropriate learning environment, including all resources within the continuum of services beyond the classroom, available to the handicapped child Practice in programming (integrating all available resources for the exceptional student) will be included.

title: ADV BEHAV MGMT EXCEP CHILD
description: Provides training in the assessment of behavioral problems and in offering suggestions for the development of appropriate  management procedures to other professionals.  Additionally, the course will focus on developing an Individualized Education Program which incorporates behavior-modification goals and  assessment.

title: TEACH OF MNTL HANDICAP
description: Appraises the objectives, methods, materials and curriculum content in arithmetic, science and social studies for the  educable and trainable retardate.

title: INTRO TO ESE
description: Presents an overview of the physical, mental, emotional, and social exceptionalities in children and their educational and  social implications.

title: NATURE&NEEDS OF STU W/AUTISM
description: Explores the history of autism, as well as neurological, cognitive, and environmental theories. Focuses on the educational characteristics of students with autism and related disabilities (i.e., Autistic Spectrum Disorders - ASD). Describes the social interaction, social communication, and imagination impairments of students with autism. Presents issues of identification and referral to special education. A minimum of eight (8) hours field experience required.

title: TECHNOLOGY APPL EXCEP STUDENT EDUCATION
description: Prepares Exceptional Student Education teachers to use the unique strength of computers for diagnostic remediation and record keeping in the remedial education environment.

title: ASSESSMENT & DIAGNOSIS ASD
description: Proposes educational assessment and diagnostic procedures to provide an objective data base that is used for individualizing instruction for children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). A minimum of eight (8) hours field experience required.

title: SEMINAR SPEC ESE
description: Affords opportunities for students to explore the specific exceptionality area(s) students need.

title: WORKING WITH FAMILIES AND COMMUNITIES IN PREKINDERGARTEN SPECIAL EDUCATION
description: Focuses on the environments in which the  exceptional infant and child must function.   Family collaboration and support, including  family systems theory and family-centered  practice, are emphasized.  In addition to the  home, school, and community, available resources  and

title: PRACTICUM IN ESE
description: Applies knowledge, strategies, and skills relevant to exceptional students in the classroom. Experiences will be tailored to each candidate's area of specialization.  Field-based experience under the supervision of a University instructor.

title: SCHOOL LAW/ESE STUDENT
description: Familiarizes student with the necessary competencies in dealing with federal, state and local laws as they pertain to Exceptional Student education.  Current application of these laws will  be reviewed.

title: CURRICULUM DESIGN/ESE
description: Examines and evaluates curriculum models, including the administration of instructional systems and curriculum materials necessary for students with disabilities. Appropriate curriculum designs based on federal laws, appropriation, staffing, and Individualized Education programs will be explored. This course provides foundational information and competencies regarding the nature and needs of students with mild disabilities. Field experience required.

title: CONTINUOUS ENROLLMENT
description: Continuous Enrollment is required every fall & spring semester and is optional every semester and is optional every summer until an "IP" (In Progress) grade has been converted to a letter grade.

title: SEMINAR IN ESE
description: Focuses on advanced theory and research in exceptional student  education.  Investigates advanced work in social and  psychological research about persons with learning disabilities and/or mental retardation and/or emotional handicaps, and/or  giftedness.  Students focus on advanced theory and research in their area of expertise.

title: FUTURE TRENDS/ISS ESE EDU
description: Explores and analyzes divergent perspectives about current and future trends and issues in Exceptional Student Education, including innovative programs, inclusion, placement, labeling, future funding, and controversial issues with regard to the effectiveness of Special Education.

title: INSTR STRAT FOR STU W/AUTISM
description: Surveys strategies appropriate for students with Autism, particularly the use of assistive and instructional technology, as well as natural, alternative and augmentative communication systems. A minimum of eight (8) hours field experience required.

title: FUTURE TRENDS/ISSUES ESE
description: Explores and analyzes future trends and issues in Exceptional Student Education, including innovative programs involving decision making, mainstreaming, training for vocational opportunities, placements, middle schools, future funding and controversial issues with regard to the administration and  supervision of Exceptional Student Education programs.

title: DISSERTATION
description: Culminates the research of the doctoral program; methods of inquiry must be appropriate to the problem being investigated.

title: DIRECTED RESEARCH
description: Investigates a significant problem in education with an emphasis in the student's area of specialization culminating in a project describing the research.

title: DISSERTATION SEMINAR
description: Assesses researchable questions in leadership and administration in a student's area of specialization resulting in a dissertation proposal

title: CURRENT ISSUES HIGHER ED
description: Explores those issues of a local, state, national, and  international nature that impact the policies, personnel and  general day to day administration of an institution of higher education.

title: STUDENT AFFAIRS PRACTICE HIGHER EDU
description: Overviews the fundamental concepts, organization, and administration of higher education student affairs/service work. Current methods and instruments as well as professionalism and ethics are discussed.

title: HISTORY OF HIGHER EDUCATION
description: Traces the historical and philosophical roots of higher education in America.  Emphasis is given to European contributions to the  ideas of the American college, the evolution and diversification  of American higher education and the pattern of higher education as it evolved through the 20th century.

title: ADMINISTRATION HIGHER ED
description: Analysis of colleges and universities as social organizations  with special emphasis on issues of administration, organization and governance of higher education.

title: DIVERSITY & COLLEGE STUDENT DEVELOPMENT
description: This survey course will expose participants to many of the theories of development that inform student affairs practices within the higher  education administration context.  As such,  students will become acquainted with the four categories of college student development theory which include: 1) cognitive structural,  2) psychosocial, 3) typological, and 4) person-environment.  Students will have taken at least two Higher Education Administration courses as prerequisites toward developing an  understanding of the HED/Student Affairs  context and, thus, application of the theories in community colleges or universities.

title: LEGAL ISSUES & COLLEGE STUDENT
description: Analyzes the legal structure of higher education including religion, academic freedom, employment, due process, student's right's, desegregation, tort liability, and other current issues.

title: ADM&SUPB ESE STU EDU PROG
description: Designed to develop knowledge and mastery of competencies necessary in special education administration. Focuses on the administrative roles in the organization and operation of exceptional education programs as well as management issues in the delivery of effective programs, while emphasizing the administrator's ability to work in inclusive settings.

title: SCHOOL LAW&THE ESE STU
description: Designed to provide in-depth information about the laws pertaining to exceptional student education and their appropriate application. Students become familiar with the necessary competencies in dealing with federal and state laws as they pertain to persons with disabilities.

title: PRACTICUM IN HIGHER EDU
description: Provides opportunity for general experience in the field of higher education.  This practicum for gives the student a variety of experiences in areas of higher education that would be new  to the individual who is currently working in the field.

title: CONDUCT EMPIRICAL STUDIES SPEC EDUC
description: Provides students with an opportunity to conduct empirical research in Exceptional Student Education/Special Education, utilizing a variety of designs, including quantitative, qualitative, and single subject designs.  Required for all Ph.D. students in Leadership and Education with a Specialization in Exceptional Student Education (ESE).  Prerequisites: ESE 770, ESE 785, HSE 703, HSE 705 and HSE 707

title: CONTINUOUS ENROLLMENT
description: Continuous Enrollment is required every fall & spring semester and is optional every semester and is optional every summer until an "IP" (In Progress) grade has been converted to a letter grade.

title: INTERN IN HIGHER EDU
description: Provides opportunity for general experience in the field of  higher education.  This internship would give the student a  variety of experiences in those areas of higher education that would be new to the individual who may be currently working in  the field.

title: CONTINUOUS MATRICULATION
description: Enrollment is required after the completion of ESE 798 and 799 each semester until the dissertation is successfully defended.

title: EXPERIENTIAL LEARNING PORTFOLIO PROJECT
description: The Occupational Therapy Program is committed to transformative education of adult learners through non-traditional lifelong learning experiences. We recognize the value of life and professional experiences and offer certified clinicians the opportunity to submit a reflective portfolio evidencing their acquired knowledge and skills that support the program's professional and educational outcomes in the areas of leadership, research and specialized advanced practice. Students may earn up to 6 credits towards their doctorate degree in occupational therapy. The transcription of portfolio credits by the university registrar is in accordance with university  policies and procedures and with the criteria outlined in the SACS accreditation handbook.

title: SEMINAR IN HIGHER ED
description: Provides students the opportunity to pursue an integrative experience, under the direction of an instructor, an area of interest in higher education administration leadership. Students will share findings in a seminar setting.

title: DISSERTATION IN PRACTICE SEMINAR
description: This is the first of two courses that together provide a field-based action research learning opportunity to analyze a problem of practice within the student's organizational context using an improvement science process toward developing meaningful solutions. This course should be a demonstration of the student's ability to perform disciplined inquiry in accordance with traditional standards for doctoral level performance. Prerequisite: Comprehensive Exam.

title: AFRICAN AMERICAN HISTORY SINCE RECONSTRUCTION
description: A political, social, cultural and economic history of African Americans since 1877. Focuses on segregation, civil rights, the family, northern migration and cultural contributions. Prerequisite: HIS 150 or 202.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF HIGHER ED ADMINISTRATION
description: Analysis of colleges and universities as social organizations with special emphasis on issues of administration, organization, and leadership in higher education.

title: TCH SOC SCI GRADES 6-12
description: Method of teaching social studies, emphasizing the integration of history, a survey of problems confronting secondary school  social studies teachers, including an evaluation of courses and textbooks; instruction in the use of audio-visual materials.

title: PEOPLE ANALYTICS & AI FOR WORKPLACE INT
description: Students will gain foundational knowledge and skills in leveraging people analytics and AI in workforce intelligence to advance learning and development, optimize performance management, and drive human performance improvement. Through an HRD lens, the course explores how analytics and emerging technologies can enhance employee experience, well-being, and strategic talent development initiatives.

title: FUNDAMENTALS COACHING IN THE WORKPLACE
description: This course explores professional coaching as a performance improvement process in organizations. Students will learn key coaching theories, frameworks, and ethical principles while developing coaching skills through application. The course also examines how technology can enhance and scale coaching in the workplace.

title: WRKSHOP:HUMAN RESRCE DEV
description: Investigates special interest areas developed from student needs and community requests. Number of credits depends on individual workshop requirements.

title: DISSERTATION IN PRACTICE
description: This is the second of two courses that together will provide a field-based action learning opportunity to analyze problems of practice using multiple frames to develop meaningful solutions.  Similar to the dissertation, the Capstone Project should be a demonstration of the student's ability to perform disciplined inquiry and in accordance with traditional standards for doctoral performance.

title: TEACHING AND TRAINING WITH SOCIAL MEDIA
description: This course provides participants an introduction to Social Media and how to effectively utilize Social Media in a teaching or training context.  Social media is an ingrained part of today's society. Students are constantly on Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, and many more sites and apps.  This course will investigate how to utilize Social Media through "real world" examples and utilize it effectively across various modalities including e-learning and m-learning platforms.  This course will demonstrate how to access social media tools and how to develop content that can be used through multiple modalities and keep students engaged.

title: INTERNATIONALIZATION HIGHER EDUCATION
description: This course will introduce students to how the impact of globalization and the integrated world economy has influenced the internationalization of higher education as evidenced by strategic agendas on the part of universities. As such, the course will explore topics within the context of different countries and specific institutions in their attempt to respond to internationalization.  The topics include but are not limited to the following:  alternative institutional models; programmatic and curriculum development efforts; the identification of barriers--including colonial epistemologies-and subsequent reforms for elimination of these; obtaining funding and/or resources; implementing organizational change; and the framing of policies for displaced refugee students within the context of student services across institutions.

title: HIGHER EDUCATION&THE LAW
description: Analyzes the legal structure of higher education including religion, academic freedom, employment, due process, student's rights, desegregation, tort liability, and other current issues.

title: CONTINUOUS MATRICULATION
description: Enrollment is required after until the dissertation is successfully  defended.

title: TEACH&LEARN AT UNI LEVEL
description: Analyzes current teaching methods and strategies at the community college and University level. This course will explore topics such as adult learning (andragogy), societal issues dealing with gender, race, racism, ethnic, and cultural factors and their impact on teaching at the collegiate level. The course covers a variety of teaching and learning styles and gives the students an opportunity to participate in classroom teaching.

title: HUMAN RESOURCE DEV
description: Delves into the key Human Resource Development (HRD) processes, competencies, and tools required to effectively lead science-based training, performance management, and leadership development functions in organizations.

title: CHANGE & ORGANIZATION DEVELOPMENT
description: Introduces HRD students to organization development (OD) and exposes them to the foundational knowledge and skills to be able to lead organization-wide change initiatives. Students will learn how to create and apply behavioral-science-based interventions to manage the process of change in today's complex organizations.

title: LEGAL ISSUES IN HRD
description: Examines the laws that impact the human resource function. Reviews history of labor laws and emphasizes current legislation that includes but is not limited to ADA, FMLA, OSHA, Title VII, ADEA, and wrongful discharge.

title: DESIG HLTH&WELL PROGRAMS
description: Examines human learning and motivation for the purpose of creating and facilitating effective learning experiences for adults in a work-related setting. Projects are required which design the environment for effective adult learning and enhance the motivational value for task completion.

title: ADULT & ORGANIZATIONAL LEARNING
description: Examines human learning and motivation for the purpose of creating and facilitating effective learning experiences for adults in a work-related setting. Students will learn how to develop and maintain effective learning processes for organizational success.

title: TRNG FACILITN/PRESENTN SKILLS
description: The course develops knowledge and mastery of competencies and presentation skills necessary for effective training delivery and facilitation for learning, talent, and leadership development professionals. Students will lead a variety of presentations exercises culminating in the creation of an engaging learning experience.

title: FOUNDATIONS ORG. CONSULTING
description: Focuses on the fundamentals and best practices of the organizational consulting process and design and implementation of an effective performance consulting project. An emphasis is placed on the relationship-building and interpersonal skills required for a successful consulting engagement.

title: PRODUCTIVITY & QUALITY
description: Introduces the applications of productivity, teamwork, and cooperation as they apply to organizational efficiency.

title: ORGNZTNL COMM & EFFECTIVENESS
description: Develops an understanding of the process of communication in the context of individual, team, leadership, and organizational effectiveness. Topics include the communication process, effects of organizational structure on communication, and relationsdhip and conflict management.

title: PRACTICUM IN HRD
description: Requires students to combine research and consulting skills to design, develop, and implement a hands-on evidence-based project to address an HRD-related problem in an organizational setting.

title: ADVANCING WORKPLACE DEI
description: This course explores the role of HRD professionals in advancing diversity, equity, and inclusion in organizations. The course illuminates the complexities of building diverse and inclusive teams and organizations and provides students with knowledge and skills to create learning, talent, leadership, and organization development strategies in the context of diversity managetement in the workplace.

title: CONTINUOUS ENROLLMENT
description: Continuous Enrollment is required every fall & spring semester and is optional every semester and is optional every summer until an "IP" (In Progress) grade has been converted to a letter grade.

title: PRINCPLS INSTRU & DIGTL DESIGN
description: Examines the principles of designing and conducting learning experiences in organizations. Students acquire knowledge and skills to strategically use innovative e-learning technologies to impact organizational results and drive performance improvement.

title: TEAM DYNAMICS
description: Emphasizes knowledge and skills necessary to build an effective team and team relationships to carry out a work related goal. Topics include person-to-person communication, small group processes, conferencing skills, networking, and coaching.

title: PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS & IMPROVEMENT
description: Analyzes performance issues and examines techniques and methods for improving performance.

title: TALENT & CAREER DEVELOPMENT
description: Overviews the major theories and skill areas in educational planning, career development, and work motivation including understanding career decision-making, using appropriate information and assessment techniques and applying knowledge and skills to the planning and conducting of career development activities in appropriate HRD settings. Resume development and job-seeking skills are taught.

title: HRD INTERNSHIP
description: Required of all students who are not currently working in the field. Students will engage in supervised field experience(s) in an HRD setting. Regular meetings with faculty and field supervisors are required. Experiences in instructional design, training, career development, program planning, or administration may be assigned. This course may be waived for students currently working in the field.

title: ORGANIZATIONAL LEARNING
description: Studies psychological and organizational paradigms associated with learning of a collective whole. Examines the implications and challenges for learning brought about by the changing nature of work and global competition. Focuses on processes and procedures for achieving organizational learning through information distribution and interpretation, making meaning and organizational memory.

title: DISSERTATION IN PRACTICE SEMINAR
description: This is the first of two capstone courses that together will provide a field-based action learning opportunity to analyze problems of practice using multiple frames to develop meaningful solutions. Similar to the dissertation, the Capstone Project should be a demonstration of the student's ability to perform disciplined inquiry in accordance with traditional standards for doctoral performance.

title: DISSERTATION SEMINAR
description: Assesses researchable questions in leadership and administration in a student's area of specialization resulting in a dissertation proposal.

title: CONTINUOUS MATRICULATION
description: Enrollment is required after the completion of ADL 798 and 799 each semester until the dissertation is successfully defended.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF HRD
description: Provides a comprehensive overview of human resource development as a discipline and a professional field of practice and aims at developing a deeper understanding of the underlying theories, processes, and practices of HRD. Provides a foundational framework for viewing problems of practice through an HRD lens.

title: DISSERTATION
description: Culminates the research of the doctoral program; methods of inquiry must be appropriate to the problem being investigated. Prerequisite: HRD 798.

title: PRINCIPLES HEALTH SERVICES
description: This course provides an overview of organizational theory and the conceptual basis of traditional managerial and executive leadership roles in health care facilities. Emphasis will be placed on the application of theory and best practice standards to the demands of the business environment. Special emphasis will be placed on the development of leadership competencies and skills through the selection of a broad range of specialty topics including: The health system, strategic planning, organizational structure, organizational performance, organizational change, motivation, leadership, managerial problem solving, organizational communication and motivation. Students will gain an understanding of the organizational structure, function and processes of health care organizations in the USA. There will be an emphasis on financial and accounting functions in health care as well as a close look at organizational theory within health care systems. A systems perspective enables the student to understand and work with complex health care organizations and their changing environment. Students will develop an understanding of the requisite skills needed by health care executives, the role and functions of management, work process, organization structure and function, their interrelationships with human factors, as well as methods for assessing organizational performance. Finally, students will learn about governance in the health care system and how organizations develop their missions.

title: HEALTH CARE POLICY
description: This course provides a critical overview of health policy, its development, implementation with emphasis on existing government programs and evolving changes. Emphasis will be placed on understanding the important health care policy issues of our times and how these policies drive the economics of the entire health care system in the United States. This course builds on the students knowledge of theories and concepts gained in HAS 510 Principles of Health Services Administration. This course is designed to provide an understanding of the complex policy dynamics and challenges of an industry in a constant state of flux. Through readings, lectures, discussions, projects and case analysis students will learn fundamental principles of policy making. They will learn how to better utilize appropriate and effective management skills in their own organization.

title: HRD CONSULTING PRACTICES
description: This course is designed to enhance your HRD consulting skills from the prospective of both the individual consultant and the organization. The goal of the course is to provide you with a practical understanding and application of the practices and processes used by HRD consultants.

title: HEALTHCARE SYSTEMS AND ADMINISTRATION
description: This course provides an overview of organizational theory and the conceptual basis of traditional managerial and executive leadership roles in the U.S. healthcare system. Emphasis will be placed on the application of theory and best practice standards to common daily operations of select healthcare organizations. Broad topical discussions include: health system infrastructure and financing, policy development, ethical standards, performance metrics, organizational change, and effective communication strategies.

title: PROGRAM EVAL IN HRD
description: Examines the principles, practices and research of training program evaluations. Prepares students to design and conduct evaluation of HRD interventions.

title: HEALTH LAW AND ETHICS
description: The provision of health care services is one of the most regulated industries in the United States. As the law and health care delivery become more interconnected, it is essential for those in the industry to keep pace with the significant changes in health law and their impact on the health care system. A strong background in health regulation is therefore becoming increasingly relevant to both clinical practice and to organizational management.  Completion of this course will provide a basic understanding of such legal issues as civil liabilities including medical malpractice, professional fees splitting, self-referrals, and licensing, administrative and corporate law/governance pertaining to health services.  Appropriate health administration requires not only understanding of applicable laws and regulations, but of the distinct ethical and social issues presented by the provision of healthcare services. Administrators, health professionals, patients and even communities as a whole are stakeholders in the ethical provision of health services. Qualified administrators and managers therefore are able to competently focus not only on the legal and business aspects of their field, but also on the social and behavioral aspects of the health administration from an ethical standpoint. This course therefore provides a practical and applicable framework to analyze the many complex issues and competing interests that arise in the modern healthcare industry.

title: QUALITY ASSURANCE
description: Analysis of theory, methods and evaluation for managing quality programs in all health care organizations.  Provides methods to assure continuous improvement in the quality of services  provided.  In depth comparison of determination for health care need, cost, and payment in selected reimbursement systems such as Medicare, Medicaid, Workers' Compensation and managed care systems.

title: SEMINAR IN HRD
description: Examines current research issues, theories and models in HRD and implications for practitioners. Students will lead discussions and present papers on topics of their primary interest. Prerequisites: HRD 711, HRD 719, HRD 728, and HRD 644, or permission of advisor and instructor.

title: HEALTH CARE ECONOMICS
description: This course provides a critical overview of the delivery and economics of health care, with emphasis on existing government programs and evolving changes.  Emphasis will be placed on understanding the important economic issues of our times and how these theories drive the economics of the entire health care system in the United States. This course builds on students' knowledge of theories and concepts gained in HSA 510 Principles of Health Services Administration and HSA 525 Health Care Policy which are both prerequisites. BUS 513 Economics Workshop will also be required for students who have not taken an undergraduate Economics course. This course is designed to provide an understanding of the complex economic forces, dynamics and challenges of an industry in a constant state of flux. Through readings, lectures, discussions, projects and case analysis, students will learn fundamental principles of strategic planning. They will learn how to better utilize appropriate and effective management skills in their own organization. Prerequisites: HSA 510 and HSA 525

title: DISSERTATION IN PRACTICE
description: This is the second of two capstone courses that together will provide a field-based action learning opportunity to analyze problems of practice using multiple frames to develop meaningful solutions. Similar to the dissertation, the Capstone Project should be a demonstration of the student's ability to perform disciplined inquiry and in accordance with traditional standards for doctoral performance.

title: LONG TERM CARE FACILITY MANAGEMENT
description: This course provides an examination of management policies and practices in the administration of long-term care facilities designed for the rapidly growing senior population. Emphasis will be placed on the application of theory and best practice standards to the demands of the business environment. Special emphasis will be placed on the development of leadership competencies and skills through the selection of specialty topics unique to this healthcare industry segment. These include licensure, planning, organizing marketing, staffing and directing complex, multicultural health care organizations, which serve a geriatric population with specific needs.

title: FIN/DEL OF HLTH CARE SER
description: This course examines the complex financial systems within today's healthcare environment and provides an understanding of the basics of health insurance and public funding programs, managed care contracting, and how services are paid. This gives the student an appreciation for the complexity of reimbursement systems and an understanding of the profound impact they have had on providers and payers, consumers, public policy makers, and the development of classification and information technology systems over the years. This course provides a comprehensive overview of the organization of the health care system and the proposals for system reform; it focuses on financing in the public and private sectors, gaps in the system and the integration of financing and delivery mechanisms. This course is designed to provide the student with an understanding of the health system from a general systems perspective. This perspective will enable the student to understand the complex nature of health system organization and to analyze our current situation. Students will develop an understanding of the structure and function of health systems financing, as well as the interrelationships among the component parts. The topics include the types and nature of various health system processes and institutions; how they evolved; their current status and possible future development.

title: ESSENT OF MANAGED CARE
description: A survey of the managed care continuum in the United States is presented. The concepts of risk and control are studied with particular focus on the management and organizational challenges involved with the continued growth of managed health care. This course includes such topics as managed care contracting, network strategy, integrated delivery systems, care management, disease management, quality, claims, and consumer affairs. In addition, Medicare and Medicaid are examined from a managed care perspective along with legal, operational, and regulatory environments.

title: PRACTICUM/INTERNSHIP
description: Advanced supervised field operation and experience in an approved health care administration setting. Required for students with less than five years health care management experience.  Optional for all other M.S./H.S.A. students. Prerequisites: HSA 510, 610, or permission of the Program Director.

title: ADVANCED STUDY
description: A specialized area of study with a faculty member on selected topics in which formal coursework is not offered. Assigned readings, reports, weekly discussions and examinations by arrangement with instructor. Prerequisite HSA 510 and HSA 610.

title: MANAGERIAL EPIDEMIOLOGY
description: This is a general course in managerial epidemiology and infectious disease with emphasis on current topics and applications in the Public Health arena. Addresses the behavioral models of health and disease, the social barrier to care and the effectiveness of the health system in promoting optimal health behavior in patients and health care personnel as well as the organizations in which they work. Health care managers are constantly surrounded by numbers that they need to manipulate and understand. Managing and interpreting health related indicators are important components of this course. The student is expected to gain a fundamental working knowledge of epidemiology methodology, infectious and emerging diseases and understand the influence of this field on other disciplines as well as the current and future topics facing health professionals today. A study of health and health status, and health assessment in the community is an important goal of this course.

title: ADVANCED STUDY
description: A specialized area of study with a faculty member on selected topics in which formal coursework is not offered. Assigned readings, reports, weekly discussions and examinations by arrangement with instructor. Prerequisite HSA 510 and HSA 610.

title: ADVANCED STUDY
description: A specialized area of study with a faculty member on selected topics in which formal coursework is not offered. Assigned readings, reports, weekly discussions and examinations by arrangement with instructor. Prerequisite HSA 510 and HSA 610.

title: ADVANCED STUDY
description: A specialized area of study with a faculty member on selected topics in which formal coursework is not offered. Assigned readings, reports, weekly discussions and examinations by arrangement with instructor. Prerequisite HSA 510 and HSA 610.

title: APPLIED BIOSTATISTICS
description: This course provides a survey of fundamental statistical concepts and applied techniques related to the practice of health services management. Methods of data collection, management, presentation, and descriptive and inferential statistics are included in this course, as well as research methodology theory applied to Public Health and Health Services. The course examines theoretical concepts, descriptive and analytic research methods that are used by the main disciplines engaged in research and evaluation of health status and health services.

title: HEALTH CARE LEADERSHIP
description: Provides an overview of leadership perspectives and strategies as they apply to health care settings. The course is designed to provide a working knowledge of leadership theory and processes, emphasizing the leader's human resources function in health care settings. Students will develop an understanding and appreciation of how effective health care leaders think, make decision and solve problems, and accomplish corporate strategy through maximizing employee commitment and other organizational resources. Also, the course endeavors to facilitate self-knowledge and career planning as well as skill development related to health care leadership, particularly skills in gaining commitment, coaching for performance and developing employees. Students learn about critical competencies for Health Care Executives and how to successfully leading a health care organization.

title: MEDICAL GROUP PRACTICE MGMT
description: Medical group practices are developed to assume and manage the economic risk inherent in health care. This course provides an overview of the complex processes involved in developing a viable organization, and emphasizes the application of theory and best practices to the demands of this business environment. An in-depth focus will be placed on the strategic components required to build a successful structure, the requisite ongoing operational assessments and the methodologies used to effect change.

title: QUALITY IMPROVEMENT & EVIDENCE BASED
description: This course provides a foundation for the implementation of quality management activities in the health care industry. The course examines theory, methods, and assessment tools health care managers and health professionals currently use, creating an environment in which quality can be measured and improved. The relevance of conscientious, explicit, and judicious use of current best evidence in making decisions about the care of individual patients is evaluated during this course. The course introduces the process of performance improvement as continuous and dynamic profess design, data collection, analysis, implementation, and ongoing evaluations.

title: HEALTH CARE HUMAN RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
description: The processes of managing and developing human resources, complying with professional and governmental regulations, and monitoring policies and culture of health care organization to maximize productivity, health and job satisfaction are explored. The course is designed to provide a working knowledge of a leader's human resources function in health in health care settings. Students will develop an understanding and appreciation of how health care leaders effectively and efficiently manage the human resource function within their organization. Students will complete a strategic human resources plan on a health care organization and provide hiring, compliance, performance measures, and training as some of the key strategic imperatives in human resources.

title: HEALTH CARE MARKETING
description: Managers must have an understanding of the various marketing concepts and tools that are available to them to successfully accomplish their assigned tasks. Decisions involving marketing must be based on a manager's overall understanding of the organization's products and services. Effective manager's work diligently to coordinate their areas of responsibility with their peers, who are managing other diverse areas of the organization. This course provides methods to evaluate marketing performance and productivity, analyze internal and external resources, and perform a SWOT analysis; present various models and methods for the promotion and positioning of health care services and products. It also emphasizes the importance of a marketing audit and incorporating that audit into the total marketing planning process. The course will also review the importance of marketing research and the analytical tools required to be successful. Students will also create a 20 page marketing plan as their final project and present the project to their peers.

title: HEALTH CARE BUDGETING & FIN MANAGEMENT
description: In order to properly manage their assigned areas of responsibility, managers must have an understanding of the various financial management concepts and tools that are available to them to successfully accomplish their assigned tasks.  They must be able to identify the sources of revenue and recognize the expenses that have an impact on their areas(s) of responsibility, and master the techniques associated with managing this information.  Decisions involving financial and human resources must be based on managers' overall understanding of the organization's mission, goals, and objectives.  Good managers work diligently to coordinate their areas of responsibility with their peers, who are managing other diverse areas of the organization.  This course will introduce students to the basic concepts and priniciples of financial management within healthcare organizations.  This course is designed to provide the student with an understanding of how health care administrators and managers use financial information to help guide and manage organizations.  Students will learn fundamental principles of financial and managerial accounting.  They will examine how to better utilize financial information in their own organization.

title: PHI SCI&THEORY DEV
description: A critical analysis of philosophy of science and epistemology as  applicable to theory development in the human science discipline.

title: HEALTHCARE STRATEGIC PLANNING
description: In order to properly manage their assigned areas of responsibility, managers must have an understanding of the various strategic management concepts and tools that are available to them to successfully accomplish their assigned tasks. Decisions involving strategy and marketing must be based on managers' overall understanding of the organization's mission, goals, and objectives. Effective managers work diligently to coordinate their areas of responsibility with their peers, who are managing other diverse areas of the organization. This course provides methods to evaluate organizational performance and productivity, analyze internal and external resources, and perform needs assessment. The course also presents various models and methods for planning and positioning of health care services and interpreting surveys used in health services management information systems. It also emphasizes the importance of creating a strategic plan and incorporating that plan into the total strategic planning process. This course represents the program capstone that integrates all previous courses.

title: RESEARCH APPLICATION IN HEALTH SERVICES
description: This course provides an introduction to basic methods of research and program evaluation within the healthcare context. Throughout the coursework, students learn to comprehend and critique published health services research and health program evaluations, and develop a design for a research or evaluation project. More specifically, students will identify different types of study design, including observational, pre-experimental and experimental designs, and their inherent threats to internal and external validity. The course is also designed to provide students with understandings on issues in the current health service research, such as measurement errors, data validity and reliability, and sampling methods and procedures. This course is recommended for students who will be carrying out policy research, social science research, or program impact evaluation within health delivery systems. It is also relevant to those who will apply the results of Health Services Research (HSR) done by others.

title: QUAL METH OF INQUIRY
description: A critical analysis of qualitative methods of inquiry for the human sciences to facilitate the understanding of the aims, processs, and outcomes of these methods.

title: ADVANCED STUDY
description: A specialized area of study with a faculty member on selected topics in which formal coursework is not offered. Assigned readings, reports, weekly discussions and examinations by arrangement with instructor. Prerequisite HSA 510 and HSA 610.

title: QUAN METH OF INQUIRY
description: Examine advanced competencies to conceptualize, design, execute, analyze, report and publish quantitative research that delivers new and useful knowledge.  Balances its presentations of research theory and computer based tools with applications to real world  problems.

title: ADV QUANTITATIVE INQUIRY
description: Builds on principles of measurement, design and sampling presented in HSE 707.  Students learn to code, orgainze, reduce, and analyze quantitative data, and to interpret and report results. Emphasis on a variety of common statistical procedures, the assumptions underlying each, and the criteria for selecting them.

title: QUAN METH OF INQUIRY
description: Examines advanced competencies to conceptualize, design, execute, analyze, report, and publish quantitative research that delivers  new and useful knowledge.  Balance its presentation of research theory and computer-based tools with application to real world problems.

title: PHI SCI&THEORY DEV
description: A critical analysis of philosophy of science and epistemology as  applicable to theory development in the human science disciplines

title: QUAL METH OF INQUIRY
description: A critical analysis of qualitative methods of inquiry for the human sciences to facilitate the understanding of the aims, processes, and outcomes of these methods.

title: QUANT METH ADV INQUIRY
description: Builds on principles of measurement, design and sampling  presented in HSN707.  Students learn to code, organize, reduce  and analyze quantitative data, and to interpret and report results.  Emphasis on a variety of common statistical procedures, the assumptions underlying each, and the criteria for selecting them.

title: INFORMATICS & HEALTHCARE TECHNOLOGY
description: This course examines the use of informatics and healthcare technology to support various healthcare functions. It emphasizes the health services administrator's use of information systems to integrate clinical, financial, and human resources data and to support managerial decision-making. This course focuses on the selection, management, application, and evaluation of various health care information systems.

title: CIVIL PROCEDURE
description: This course is about the process through which civil legal disputes are resolved in American federal courts. It addresses broad issues of justice and governmental power. It also deals with some very  practical questions, such as how the legal system works and the  lawyer's role within that system. Ethical and professionalism  concerns raised in conjunction with the adversarial process will also be addressed.

title: LEGAL RESEARCH AND WRITING 1
description: Legal Research and Writing 1("LRW 1") is the first of two required semesters of research and writing instruction. The primary focus of the course will be the written expression of legal analysis and its secondary focus will be legal research. The course will teach students how to draft objective legal memoranda by presenting students with facts from which they must develop rational outcomes based on researching statutes and case law, and to reduce those outcomes to writing to communicate with other lawyers and clients. In addition to the written assignments, each student will also make a short oral report to the Professor.

title: TOOLS FOR SUCCESS
description: The goal for this course is to introduce you to the fundamentals of success in law school and in your legal career. During the semester, it's expected that you begin to appreciate and integrate this foundational knowledge into your approach to learning and practicing the law.

title: CRIMINAL LAW
description: This will be a 3-credit hour introductory study of criminal law. At minimum we will cover the general principles of criminal law, such as men_s rea, causation, strict liability, mistake and punishment theory. We will also study specific crimes (criminal homicide, rape and property crimes) and some of the various justifications (e.g., self defense, necessity, duress) and excuses (e.g., insanity, intoxication) for criminal conduct. We will also examine other forms of liability, including felony murder, attempt, solicitation, conspiracy and accomplice liability

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN HEALTH SERVICES ADM
description: This course is designed as an integrative experience for those students with a strong healthcare management background. It requires students to utilize knowledge gained in other courses and apply it to a comprehensive practical project. (Required for students with more than two years healthcare management experience. All others are required to complete the internships.)

title: CRIMINAL PROCEDURE
description: This is an introductory constitutional criminal procedure course. We will study the 4th Amendment (search and seizure) and 5th Amendment (privilege against self-incrimination) and 6th Amendment (right to counsel) through the leading cases of the United States Supreme Court and other federal and state courts, considering the implications on the process during the investigative phase of a criminal case.

title: ADV QUALITATIVE INQUIRY
description: Seminar discussions of qualitative readings to facilitate an advanced understanding of the qualitative perspective in nursing education, and social work research.  This course is specifically designed to assist students in particular methods for research and practice implications. In depth analysis distinguishes this course from first qualitative course.

title: QUAL METH OF ADV INQUIRY
description: Seminar discussions of qualitative readings to facilitate an advanced understanding of the qualitative perspective in nursing education, and social work research.  This course is specifically designed to assist students in particular methods for research an and  practice implications.  Indepth analysis distinguishes this course from first qualitative course.

title: INTRVW COUNSEL&PROB SOLV
description: This is considered to be a "skills" class and so students completing the class will have had an opportunity to practice how to conduct  an initial client interview, counsel a client seeking to make a decision on how to proceed in either a dispute or transactional setting, and several negotiation or problem-solving situations. The goal is not to perfect these skills but to leave the class having learned, considered, and discussed various concepts, styles and strategies related to interviewing, counseling and problem-solving such that, when they become lawyers, students are able to use these concepts, styles, and strategies in practice regardless of the area of law they pursue.

title: PROPERTY
description: This course introduces you to the fundamentals of property law so you can connect the dots between the common law history of property and the modern manifestation of those principles in the statutory and regulatory guidelines that control many modern transactions. During the semester, you will develop an increasingly sophisticated appreciation for the common law roots of property, and hope you begin to appreciate and integrate this foundational knowledge into your law school and professional careers.

title: TORTS
description: This course is a survey of modern tort law's most important topics. It focuses on intentional and negligent torts, vicarious liability, strict liability, products liability, medical malpractice and the defenses to and damages caused by those torts.  Students will gain an appreciation for how tort law affects societal values as it balances the interests of an increasingly diverse population and America's entrepreneurial interests. June 20 2018  9:29 AM  Julie Hagaman

title: CONSTITUTIONAL LAW
description: This course provides an introduction to the constitutional law of the United States of America. Our journey is divided roughly into two halves. The first concerns the structure of government, exploring ways by which the Constitution creates and divides national power among various actors. The second concerns the protection of individual rights and liberties, especially those guaranteed by the Bill of Rights and the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses of the 14th Amendment.

title: PRESIDENTIAL POWERS
description: This course focuses on presidential power and its limits, covering the actions of historical Presidents and the current sitting President and the executive branch. In this course, students will learn the political structures and constitutional principles that might limit or empower the presidency and how regulatory agencies operate within the executive branch. This course will rely upon historical cases and documents that helped lay the foundation for defining the parameters of the presidency, as well as new cases and issues arising out of the current political climate that give timely context for analysis and application of the constitutional and structural principles. The ordering of the course topics is malleable to allow flexibility to incorporate new cases and issues as they arise. Because some issues intertwine with other substantive areas, e.g., immigration, health care, and the environment, the course might draw upon the expertise of other faculty and guest speakers to help students understand other specialized areas and the impact of the presidency in those other substantive areas.

title: INDIVIDUAL FEDERAL INCOME TAXATION
description: This will be a 3-credit hour introductory study of the federal income tax. We will cover general tax components including income,  adjustments, deductions, and credits. In addition we will cover  transactions such as the sale of real property and transfers between spouses incident to divorce, as well as, concepts such as the  assignment of income and accounting. We will also cover income tax return preparation using commercial tax preparation software  (TaxSlayer).

title: LEGAL RESEARCH AND WRITING 2
description: Legal Research and Writing 2 ("LRW 2") is the second of two required semesters of research and writing instruction. LRW 2 continues instruction in legal research, legal writing, and legal analysis begun in the fall semester. This second semester course is a graded, three-credit hour course of instruction that continues with a focus on writing skills, legal analysis, and improved legal research skills as used by the advocate. The emphasis will be on writing documents that utilize persuasive, as opposed to objective, argument.

title: EVIDENCE
description: This course will focus on the Federal Rules of Evidence (FRE) with some references to the Florida Rules of Evidence where they differ from the FRE. We will study the basic rules, including those dealing with relevancy, character evidence, hearsay, opinion evidence, and authentication. The course will also cover making and meeting  objections, preserving the record for appeal, and the limitations the Sixth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution imposes on the use of hearsay evidence in criminal trials.

title: CONTRACTS
description: This course is an introduction to the law of contracts, and will cover the sources of law for contract cases, the principal situations  promises are enforced, contract formation, defenses and excuses, breach, remedies, and third-party rights and duties.

title: CREATING THE CONSTITUTION
description: This course will afford students the opportunity to gain a developed understanding of the genesis of the U.S. Constitution.  To set the context, the course will draw upon the intellectual traditions available in the late eighteenth century.  Then the course will consider the rhetorics that relate to the Constitutional Convention, state ratifications, and the Bill of Rights.  Class discussion will address how the various ideas present at the formation of the Constitution have influenced constitutional law over the centuries.

title: ELECTION LAW
description: This course examines the practice of democracy throughout the United States. We will read several of the major Supreme Court cases involving the constitutional right to vote, including issues related to ballot access, minority vote dilution, gerrymandering, the regulation of political parties, campaign finance, and election administration. We will also examine the impact that federal and state laws have in shaping the design of state electoral systems, particularly after Bush v. Gore.

title: ACCOUNTING FOR LAWYERS
description: An introduction to the basic accounting principles, procedures, procedures, and the accounting cycle. Introduction to the environment of accounting with an overview of the general purpose financial statements. The course seeks to make students familiar with accounting vocabulary and concepts, conversant with the structure and meaning of financial statements, and introduce them to the basics of analyzing financial information.

title: BUSINESS ORGANIZATIONS
description: This course surveys the law of modern business organizations. You will be introduced to the laws governing corporations, as well as  other business forms. Topics include choice of business form;  principles of agency law; the rights, duties, and powers of partners, members, shareholders, directors, and officers; the role of corporate managers; shareholders' rights; and the relationship between state and federal law.

title: COMMERCIAL LAW OVERVIEW
description: This course is a study of the law which defines the relationship between sellers and buyers of goods and creditors and debtors when security interests in personal property are granted.

title: ADMINISTRATIVE LAW
description: This course will study how federal government agencies make and apply law and will introduce the institutions, procedures, and  theories of the administrative state. The course will cover a variety of topics, including the legal framework (both constitutional and  statutory) that governs administrative action; the role of agencies in interpreting statutory and regulatory law; and judicial review of  agency decisions. Like Civil Procedure in the first-year curriculum, this course is not about the substantive rules that govern particular activities; rather, it is about legal institutions and the procedures  they, and private parties interacting with them, must use.

title: TRIAL ADVOCACY
description: This course is taught in ?workshop? format where students actually practice the skills needed to successfully represent a client in a jury trial. Students will learn effective skills for jury selection, opening statement, direct and cross examination of witnesses (both lay and expert), objections and closing argument. Additionally, students will focus on case theory development and strategies best suited to jury persuasion. The final exam consists of a full simulated jury trial. Prerequisite: Evidence.

title: PROFESSIONAL RESPONSIBIL
description: This course is designed to acquaint you with the standards for lawyer conduct and liability. The class will focus on the obligations of a lawyer -both as an individual and as a member of the legal profession. Although these obligations are defined in ethical rules and other codes of conduct, the rules serve as a floor for professional conduct. A lawyer's work largely turns upon relationships. As such professional responsibility reflects how the lawyer's conduct affects others -such as clients, colleagues, the courts, and society. Being a professional lawyer is not only following rules, it is building a reputation that reflects positively on you and the legal profession.

title: DIRECTED RESEARCH
description: a paper written for a graded directed research project (a two-credit directed research project requires a thirty (30) page paper and must also meet the word requirement minimum of 7,500) under the supervision of a full-time faculty member. Students must be in good academic standing to be eligible for directed research.  The supervising professor must approve the paper as fulfilling the Requirement.

title: COMMERCIAL LAW TRIAL ADVOCACY
description: This course is taught in a workshop format where students actually practice the skills needed to prepare and represent a client in a commercial case. Students will learn how to initially interview the client and witnesses, prepare pleadings, conduct motion practice, take discovery, pretrial practice and actually conduct a commercial jury trial. The emphasis will be on a commercial law case and will satisfy the skills requirement. There is a cap on this class. Prerequisites: Commercial Law and Evidence.

title: NATURAL RESOURCES SEMINAR
description: The Natural Resource Seminar is a foundational survey course concerning the laws and policies related to management of natural resources. Natural resources include public lands, timber, fisheries, water, oil, natural gas and minerals, wetlands, forests, rangelands, wildlife and vegetation (including biodiversity), ecosystems, and even climate.  Conservation law and policy and geography are both important considerations in the study of natural resources.  The resources typically exist in particular places, with each place having its own ecological, socio-cultural, political, aesthetic, economic, and moral and/or spiritual characteristics.  Conservation creates a fundamental tension between use and consumption of natural resources and the protection and preservation of nature for future generations. The course introduces differences in philosophical viewpoints between anthropocentric and ecocentric systems and examines whether conventional conservation measures promote true sustainability and healthy, diverse, and robust ecosystems. This course makes substantial use of cases and problems to put natural resource issues in the context of their complex, place-based characteristics to explore real-world implications beyond the level of pure abstraction.  To further develop depth of understanding, students will choose a topic of interest and write a twenty to thirty page research paper (thirty pages minimum if writing to try to satisfy the Upper Level Writing Requirement).  Students will also present the results of their research in a thirty-minute presentation to the class.  Because the class depends on student interests, each student who enrolls will be able to focus on a subject of particular interest to that student; as a necessary corollary, the subject matters of emphasis will correspond to the areas where students are most interested. t

title: INTERNATIONAL LAW SEMINAR
description: This is an introduction to the broad field of international law by examining: the sources of international law: several of the fora that may address international law questions; the actors that have rights and duties under international law; and questions concerning the efficacy of international law. Many of the international law sources and fora are different from what you may have met thus far in law school, i.e. different from cases and statutes as sources, national courts as fora. I urge you to compare and contrast international legal sources and processes to those national law sources and processes with which you are already familiar. By the end of Chapter 5 - a good place to begin to pull the course together, you should be able to say what international law is, how it differs from national law and (to some degree) why and in what situations international law actually "works". A second goal of the course is to familiarize you , through the materials in the casebook with the various "raw materials" with which lawyers who face international legal issues must work. While pursing these goals, we will learn about many topics that fall under the broad heading "international law". These include foreign investments, human rights, the use of force, international air transportation, diplomatic immunity, the United Nations, extradition and many more issues.

title: BUSINESS PLANNING
description: This is a practical hands-on course devoted to the many aspects of planning for a business. Students in this paper course will individually and on a team basis: 1) Plan and draft legal documents at business inception (using Florida law); 2) Plan and draft legal documents for raising capital, including private offering memoranda; 3) Plan and draft legal documents for the sale of a business, including letter of intent, confidentiality agreement, and acquisition agreement; 4) Plan and draft legal documents for business liquidation and sale of assets. The course objective is to be competent as a practitioner in handling business issues that arise from privately held corporate entities and other such entities.

title: FLORIDA FAMILY LAW MOTION PRACTICE
description: This course would focus on drafting motions in the pre litigation and litigation stages of child and family law cases including divorce, delinquency and dependency, alimony, child support and property division and all other motions.

title: INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY
description: This course will explore and  survey legal issues associated with U.S. Intellectual Property Law. Among other things, we will investigate the parameters of copyright, patent, and trademark law. We will discuss what works are  protected by Intellectual Property, what rights are granted to the  Intellectual Property owner for how long, what constitutes  Intellectual Property infringement, and defenses to infringement  actions.

title: COLLABORATIVE FAMLY CLINIC
